427	\begin{lemma}\leanok
428	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
429	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
430	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
431	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
432	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
433	  Let $W$, $Z$ be schemes with $W$ \emph{reduced}, $W$ a \emph{Jacobson space}, and $Z$
434	  \emph{separated}. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z$ be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
435	  $x \in W$, the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
436	  $\Spec \kappa(x) \to W$ (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
437	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$), then $g_1 = g_2$.
438	\end{lemma}
439	
440	\begin{proof}
441	  This is the ``dense closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
442	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
443	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
444	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
445	  dominant probe.
446	
447	  Form the coproduct $P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)$ and the morphism
448	  $\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W$ defined by $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$ of the residue-field points
449	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$. Its topological range contains every closed point of $W$:
450	  the inclusion $\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P$ composes with $\mathrm{probe}$ to
451	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ (the defining property of $\mathtt{Sigma.desc}$), and the
452	  range of $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x$ is the singleton $\{x\}$
453	  ($\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}$). Because $W$ is a Jacobson space, the closed points
454	  are dense ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$, i.e.\ $\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W$), so
455	  the range of $\mathrm{probe}$ is dense and $\mathrm{probe}$ is \emph{dominant}.
456	
457	  Componentwise, $g_1$ and $g_2$ agree after $\mathrm{probe}$: precomposing with each coproduct
458	  inclusion $\iota_x$ reduces (by $\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}$) to
459	  $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2$,
460	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point $x$. By the universal property of the coproduct
461	  ($\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}$) this gives $\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2$.
462	  Since $\mathrm{probe}$ is dominant and $Z$ is separated, $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ cancels it
463	  on the left to yield $g_1 = g_2$.
464	\end{proof}

528	\begin{theorem}
529	[Theorem of the cube]
530	  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
531	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Theorem (of the cube), Ch.~II \S6 ``The theorem of the cube: I'', book p.~55 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 66)
532	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM. Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties, $Z$ any variety and $x_0$,

535	  % $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$ and $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, $L$ is trivial."

545	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
546	
547	\begin{proposition}
548	
549	\leanok
550	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
551	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
552	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
553	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
554	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
555	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
556	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
557	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
558	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
559	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
560	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
561	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
562	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
563	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
564	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
565	  single $\bar k$-point.
566	\end{proposition}
567	
568	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
569	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
570	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
571	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
572	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
573	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
574	\begin{proof}
575	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
576	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
577	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.
578	
579	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
580	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
581	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
582	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
583	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).
584	
585	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
586	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
587	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
588	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
589	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
590	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
591	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
592	  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
593	  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
594	  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
595	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
596	  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
597	  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
598	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)
599	
600	  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
601	  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
602	  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
603	  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
604	  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
605	  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
606	\end{proof}
607	
608	\begin{remark}
609	  \label{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}
610	  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
611	  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
612	  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of
613	  Proposition~3.10 and which proves that maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to
614	  translation. But the \emph{single-curve} base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' is
615	  not a formal consequence of the Rigidity Lemma by itself; in characteristic-free form it
616	  rests on \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}. This refines the iter-156 expectation that a single
617	  $\mathbb P^1$ factor would be cube-free: the multi-factor \emph{induction} is cube-free
618	  (it is pure Rigidity-Lemma additivity), but the base of that induction is not. See the
619	  Strategy-modifying findings of this chapter's writer report.
620	\end{remark}

622	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
623	
624	\begin{proposition}
625	
626	\leanok
627	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
628	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
629	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
630	  \uses{def:genus}
631	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
632	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
633	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
634	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
635	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
636	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
637	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
638	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
639	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
640	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
641	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
642	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
643	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
644	\end{proposition}
645	
646	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
647	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
648	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
649	\begin{proof}
650	  \uses{def:genus}
651	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
652	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
653	
654	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
655	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
656	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
657	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
658	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
659	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
660	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
661	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
662	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
663	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
664	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
665	\end{proof}
666	
667	\begin{remark}
668	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
669	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
670	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
671	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
672	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
673	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
674	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
675	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
676	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
677	\end{remark}

679	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
680	
681	\begin{theorem}
682	
683	\leanok
684	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
685	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
686	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
687	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
688	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
689	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
690	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
691	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
692	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
693	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
694	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
695	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
696	
697	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
698	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
699	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
700	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
701	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
702	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
703	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
704	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
705	  \eta[A]$.
706	\end{theorem}
707	
708	\begin{proof}
709	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
710	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
711	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
712	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
713	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
714	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
715	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
716	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
717	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
718	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
719	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
720	
721	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
722	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
723	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
724	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
725	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
726	\end{proof}

230	  set wU : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar) :=
231	    U.ι ≫ (X ⊗ Y).hom with hwU
232	  set px : Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar) ⟶ (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme :=
233	    pointOfClosedPoint wU x hxc with hpx
234	  -- Reduce the residue-field-probe goal to the `k̄`-point statement by cancelling the iso
235	  -- `e := Spec.map (residueFieldIsoBase …).hom` on the left (`e ≫ U.fromSpecResidueField x = px`).
236	  rw [← cancel_epi (Spec.map (residueFieldIsoBase wU x hxc).hom)]
237	  suffices h : px ≫ U.ι ≫ Over.Hom.left f =
238	      px ≫ U.ι ≫ Over.Hom.left (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f) by
239	    rw [hpx] at h
240	    simpa only [pointOfClosedPoint, Category.assoc] using h
241	  -- `q := px ≫ U.ι : Spec k̄ ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left` is the `k̄`-rational point at the closed point `x`;
242	  -- it is a *section* of the structure map `(X ⊗ Y).hom` (a genuine `k̄`-point of `X ⊗ Y`).
243	  set q : Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar) ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left := px ≫ U.ι with hq
244	  have hqsec : q ≫ (X ⊗ Y).hom = 𝟙 _ := by
245	    rw [hq, Category.assoc]; exact pointOfClosedPoint_comp wU x hxc
246	  -- Rewrite the collapsed side `(retract ≫ f).left = retract.left ≫ f.left`.
247	  rw [Over.comp_left]
248	  -- It remains to prove the `k̄`-point slice-constancy
249	  --   `q ≫ f.left = q ≫ retract.left ≫ f.left`,
250	  -- i.e. `f` agrees at the `k̄`-point `q = (x_X, y)` and its `X`-collapse `retract(q) = (x₀, y)`.
251	  -- Both points lie on the proper integral slice `X_y` over the `k̄`-point `y := q ≫ p₂`, which
252	  -- (saturation `_hUV`) lies entirely inside `U`, hence (`_hfU`) maps under `f` into the affine
253	  -- `U₀`. The deep content "a proper integral `k̄`-scheme into an affine is constant on
254	  -- `k̄`-points" is now-proven as `eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral`: realising the slice
255	  -- as `X` via the section `s := lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ ŷ)` over the `k̄`-point `ŷ : 𝟙_ ⟶ Y`
256	  -- lifting `y`, one
257	  -- corestricts `(s ≫ f).left : X.left → Z.left` to `U₀.toScheme` and applies the sub-lemma to the
258	  -- two `k̄`-points `q ≫ p₁` and `x₀.left` of the proper integral `X`. The residual geometric
259	  -- assembly (the section `ŷ`/`s`, the integrality of the slice as a retract of the integral
260	  -- `X ⊗ Y`, the two pullback-`hom_ext` point identities `q = (q ≫ p₁) ≫ s` and
261	  -- `retract(q) = x₀.left ≫ s`, and the `IsOpenImmersion.lift` corestriction) is the chain's lone
262	  -- remaining deep `sorry`; the algebraic heart is discharged.
263	  sorry
264	
265	/-- **Bridge 2 of the Rigidity Lemma (slice-constancy / the agreement equation), the residual
266	geometric input.** Let `X` be complete (proper) over an algebraically closed `k̄`, `x₀` a
267	`k̄`-point of `X`, and `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` into a separated `Z`. Let `U = p₂⁻¹(V)` be a `p₂`-saturated
268	open of `X ⊗ Y` (the preimage of a set `Vset ⊆ Y`) on which `f` lands inside a single **affine**
269	open `U₀ ⊆ Z`. Then `f` agrees on `U` with the collapsed map `retract ≫ f`
270	(`retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`, i.e. `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)`):
271	
272	  `U.ι ≫ f.left = U.ι ≫ (retract ≫ f).left`.
273	
274	This is the cohomology-free **route B** of the iter-159 `mathlib-analogist` consult
275	(`analogies/rigidity-affineconst.md`); it is the genuinely-deep residual of the Rigidity-Lemma
276	chain (analogist estimate ≈ 1–2 further iterations) and is therefore isolated here as a named
277	top-level obligation with a precise statement and `sorry` body. The relative Stein-factorisation /
278	proper-pushforward `f_*𝒪 = 𝒪` framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap and is **deliberately avoided**.
279	
280	The intended proof (no coherent cohomology):
281	1. *Per closed slice.* For each closed point `y ∈ Vset`, `κ(y) = k̄` (`[IsAlgClosed kbar]`, finite
282	   type). Saturation puts the whole fibre `X_y` inside `U`, so `f` maps the proper integral slice
283	   `X_y ≅ X` into the affine `U₀`. By `isField_of_universallyClosed` +
284	   `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` + alg-closedness, `Γ(X_y) = k̄`, so the slice maps to a
285	   single `k̄`-point of `U₀`
286	   (`ext_of_isAffine`); that point is `f(x₀, y)`, since `(x₀, y) ∈ X_y`. Hence `f` and `retract ≫ f`
287	   agree at every closed point of `U`.
288	2. *Globalise.* Closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type `k̄`-scheme `U`
289	   (`closure_closedPoints`, the Jacobson-space property). Turning "agrees at each closed point"
290	   into one dominant probe (the coproduct `∐_{x∈closedPoints U} Spec κ(x) ⟶ U`, dense range) and
291	   feeding it to `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'` (the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
292	   `rigidity_core` already uses) yields the morphism equality on all of `U`. This last
293	   "dense-closed-points ⟹ hom-ext" connective is the one piece Mathlib does not package directly. -/
294	theorem rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine
295	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
296	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
297	    [IsProper X.hom]
298	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ⊗ Y).hom]
299	    [LocallyOfFiniteType (X ⊗ Y).hom]
300	    [IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]
301	    [IsSeparated Z.hom]
302	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
303	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X)
304	    (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens)
305	    (Vset : Set Y.left)

# Analogy: relative "proper-into-affine constant on fibres" as a morphism equality (Rigidity bridge 2)

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
rigidity-affineconst

## Iteration
159

## Question
Does Mathlib have an idiom for the **relative** statement "a proper morphism with
geometrically-connected fibres into an affine target is constant along fibres", expressed as a
**scheme-morphism equality**, usable to close the agreement equation of `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`
(`AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:181`)? Concretely on `U = X × V`:
`U.ι ≫ f.left = U.ι ≫ (lift (toUnit (X⊗Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left`.

## Project artifact(s)
- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:111-181` — `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`; the
  agreement-equation `sorry` at L181 is "bridge 2".
- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:243-274` — `rigidity_core` consumes it via
  `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`.

## Decisions identified

### Decision 1: the RELATIVE framing (Stein factorization / proper pushforward `f_* O_X = O_base`)

- **Mathlib idiom**: **absent.** There is no `SteinFactorization`, no proper pushforward of the
  structure sheaf with a connectedness conclusion, and no proper base change for `H⁰`. Searches:
  `lean_leansearch "Stein factorization proper morphism pushforward structure sheaf"` returns only
  `SheafOfModules.pushforward` / `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` (the bare functor `f_*`, no
  `f_* O = O` theorem) and `TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward_sheaf_of_sheaf`. `loogle` for proper
  pushforward connectedness: nothing. The relative `Γ(X ×_S T) = Γ(X) ⊗ Γ(T)` (flat base change
  for `H⁰`) is also absent.
- **Project's path (if pursued)**: build `p₂,* O_{X×V} = O_V` and factor `f` through it.
- **Gap**: divergent-and-wrong as a *first* move — this framing requires `H⁰`-base-change /
  coherent-cohomology infrastructure Mathlib does not have. Multi-iter gap-fill.
- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (do **not** pursue this framing for bridge 2).

### Decision 2: the GLOBAL-SECTIONS + per-slice field route ("route B", cohomology-free)

- **Mathlib idiom present**:
  - `AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isAffine` (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry...`, via leanfinder): a map
    into an **affine** scheme is determined by its global-sections map `appTop`. THIS is the
    "morphism into affine determined by `Γ`" handle the directive hoped for.
  - `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` + `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`
    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper`): `Γ(slice)` is a field, module-finite over the
    base field.
  - `IsAlgClosed` + finite/algebraic ⟹ trivial extension (`Mathlib.FieldTheory.IsAlgClosed.*`):
    at a **closed** point of a finite-type `k̄`-scheme the residue field is `k̄`, so
    `Γ(slice) = k̄` and the slice→affine map is a single `k̄`-point.
  - `JacobsonSpace`, `closedPoints`, `closure_closedPoints`, `LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace`
    (`Mathlib.Topology.JacobsonSpace`, `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.FiniteType`):
    closed points are **dense** in a finite-type `k̄`-scheme.
  - `ext_of_isDominant` / `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`
    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated`): two maps out of a **reduced** scheme into a
    **separated** target agreeing on a **dominant** (= dense-range, `dominant_eq_topologically`)
    subobject are equal. (Already used by `rigidity_core`.)
  - `Scheme.fromSpecResidueField x` with `range = {x}` and `IsPreimmersion`
    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ResidueField`): the per-point probe.
- **The assembly (cohomology-free)**:
  1. For each **closed** point `y ∈ V`: `κ(y) = k̄`; the slice `X_y ≅ X` is proper integral over
     `k̄`, maps into the affine `U₀`; `isField_of_universallyClosed` + `finite_appTop` + alg-closed
     ⟹ `Γ(X_y) = k̄` ⟹ (via `ext_of_isAffine`) `f|X_y = const = (retract ≫ f)|X_y` **as
     morphisms into `U₀ ⊆ Z`** (the two ring maps `Γ(U₀) → k̄` have the same kernel = the point,
     and a `k̄`-algebra map to `k̄` is pinned by its kernel).
  2. The closed slices are jointly **dense** in `U` (`closure_closedPoints`, Jacobson). Feed a
     dominant probe (coproduct of the closed-point `fromSpecResidueField`s, or any dense-range
     witness) to `ext_of_isDominant` ⟹ `f|U = (retract ≫ f)|U`.
- **The one genuinely-missing connective**: a packaged "morphisms agreeing on a **dense set of
  closed points** are equal" hom-extensionality. Mathlib has only the **single-dominant-morphism**
  `ext_of_isDominant`; turning "agrees at every closed point" into one dominant probe needs an
  (infinite, `Set`-indexed) coproduct `∐_{x∈closedPoints U} Spec κ(x) → U` plus a `DenseRange`
  proof. This is buildable from present pieces, not a cohomology gap.
- **Gap**: divergent-but-tractable. ~2–3 iter assembly, **no cohomology**.
- **Verdict**: PROCEED (this is the route; idiom = `ext_of_isAffine` per-slice +
  `ext_of_isDominant` globalize).

### Decision 3 (actionable signature point): `[IsAlgClosed kbar]` is missing from the chain

Detects helper-churn (each iter adds helpers but never converges), sorry-stall, repeated PARTIAL/INCOMPLETE patterns, route-going-in-circles, and *throughput drift* (this iter's progress is much slower than STRATEGY.md's estimate predicts). Also checks the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal for dispatch-sanity issues (excessive objective count, files known to be blocked). Renders a CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR verdict per active route with specific corrective recommendations when CHURNING or STUCK.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
11	    do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
12	    have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
13	    verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.
14	
15	    **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
16	      - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
17	        or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
18	        skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
19	      - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
20	        signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
21	        added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
22	        CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
23	      - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
24	        sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
25	        is no trajectory to extrapolate from).
26	
27	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
28	    with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
29	    open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
30	    re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
31	  - My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
32	    progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
33	    The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
34	    judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
35	    corrective.
36	
37	  ### Strict context discipline
38	
39	  Your directive must contain ONLY:
40	    - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
41	      iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
42	    - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
43	      sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
44	      (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
45	    - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
46	      and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
47	      so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
48	      K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
49	      `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
50	      whole strategy.
51	    - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
52	      this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
53	      operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
54	    - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.
55	
56	  Your directive MUST NOT include:
57	    - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
58	      soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
59	    - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
60	      reviewer's territory).
61	    - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
62	      above).
63	
64	  If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
65	  it. My value depends on the narrow focus.
66	
67	  ### Acting on my verdicts
68	
69	  Verdicts are per-route:
70	
71	  - **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
72	    prover round.
73	  - **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
74	    shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
75	    corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
76	    structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
77	    matching subagent from the catalog.
78	  - **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
79	    iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
80	  - **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
81	    data). Proceed but watch.
82	
83	  **CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
84	  respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
85	  rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
86	  Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
87	  failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.

89	
90	# Progress Critic
91	
92	You are the fresh-context progress critic. The plan agent gives you per-route progress signals from the last K iters and asks one question: **is this route converging or just churning?**
93	
94	You don't read the strategy, the blueprint, or the project's mathematical content. Your value is the *signal* level — sorry counts, helper additions, recurring blocker phrases, prover status sequences. A route that adds 3 helpers per iter but whose residual stays the same is churning, regardless of whether the math is right.
95	
96	## Stance
97	
98	You are the corrective for a known failure pattern. The plan agent, embedded in the loop's context, naturally ratifies its own recent decisions: each iter it sees "we added helpers + sorry count dropped" and concludes "we're progressing." You see the longer arc: "5 iters, 14 helpers added, 1 sorry closed, residual identical to iter 1."
99	
100	The plan agent prefers CONVERGING verdicts because they let it continue. You should NOT give that bias the benefit of the doubt. When the signals point at churn, you say CHURNING. When the signals point at stall, you say STUCK. You don't soften.
101	
102	## Directive Format
103	
104	```markdown
105	# Progress Critic Directive
106	
107	## Slug
108	<slug>
109	
110	## Iter
111	<NNN>
112	
113	## Active routes / files under review
114	
115	For each route the planner is considering for this iter's prover work, ONE block:
116	
117	### Route: <name or file path>
118	
119	- **Started at iter**: <NNN>
120	- **Iters audited**: <NNN-K to NNN-1>
121	
122	#### Sorry counts per iter
123	- iter-NNN-K: <count> (e.g. 5)
124	- iter-NNN-(K-1): <count> (e.g. 5)
125	- iter-NNN-(K-2): <count> (e.g. 4)
126	- ...
127	- iter-NNN-1: <count>
128	
129	#### Helpers added per iter
130	- iter-NNN-K: <list or count of new declarations introduced>
131	- ...
132	- iter-NNN-1: <list or count>
133	
134	#### Prover statuses per iter
135	- iter-NNN-K: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary from prover report>
136	- ...
137	- iter-NNN-1: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary>
138	
139	#### Recurring blocker phrases
140	- "<verbatim blocker phrase>" appears in iter-X, iter-Y, iter-Z reports — <one line>
141	- "<verbatim blocker phrase>" appears in iter-X, iter-Y — <one line>
142	- ...
143	
144	#### Strategy estimate vs reality
145	- **`Iters left` from STRATEGY.md** (verbatim from the relevant `## Phases & estimations` row): <e.g. "3">
146	- **Elapsed iters in current phase**: <e.g. "9">
147	- **Phase started at iter**: <e.g. "iter-117 — the iter where this phase row first appeared with its current estimate>
148	
149	#### Planner's current proposal for this iter
150	- <one paragraph: what the planner wants to assign>
151	
152	## PROGRESS.md proposal (this iter)
153	
154	The planner's `## Current Objectives` list it is about to commit. Used for the dispatch-sanity check.
155	
156	- **File count**: <N>
157	- **Files**: <comma-separated basenames>
158	- **Dispatch cap (from --max-objectives)**: <e.g. "10">
159	
160	## Out of scope
161	<routes the planner is NOT considering this iter and does not want assessed>
162	```
163	
164	## What you check
165	
166	For each route's block:
167	
168	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
169	
170	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
171	
172	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
173	
174	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
175	
176	5. **Planner's proposal.** Is the proposal "another helper round, similar to the last K iters"? If so, and your signals say churn, your verdict is CHURNING and the planner must escalate (blueprint, mathlib analogy, refactor, or pivot). If the proposal differs (refactor, blueprint expansion, route pivot), that's the planner already escalating — credit it.
177	
178	6. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
179	
180	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent. The deterministic `plan_validate` hook caps dispatch downstream of you, but you flag the *planner intent* loudly so the planner self-corrects rather than relying on the safety net every iter.
181	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter (e.g. 4 → 7 → 12) while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating. Flag this even if file count is below the cap.
182	
183	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report (see Report format).
184	
185	7. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
186	
187	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
188	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
189	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
190	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
191	
192	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature — the strategy claims K iters remain but K-many iters have already passed without closure. That's a strategic problem, not a tactical one. Surface it; the planner's corrective is either to revise STRATEGY.md's estimate to honesty or to escalate (pivot, narrow scope). "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy — every phase row should carry an estimate.
193	
194	   Note: throughput honesty is route-level — it lands in the per-route block, not the dispatch-sanity block.
195	
196	## Verdict rules
197	
198	Apply these rules verbatim:
199	
200	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
201	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
202	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
203	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
204	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with **zero prover dispatches** on this route (no `Foo.lean` ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds — re-blueprinting, re-strategizing, re-organizing — without ever firing a prover is the textbook stall. Each such iter individually shows "structural change in approach" (so the first clause fails), but the empirical signature is exactly what CHURNING was designed to flag. Use this clause when the planner is in a "we keep refactoring but never test it" pattern.
205	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters.
206	- **UNCLEAR**: route is fresh (< K iters of data) OR signals are ambiguous.
207	
208	If multiple rules match a route, pick the worse verdict (CHURNING > CONVERGING; STUCK > CHURNING).
209	
210	## Recommended actions per verdict
211	
212	For CHURNING or STUCK, your report names ONE primary corrective TYPE. The planner consults the catalog for the matching subagent.
213	
214	- **Blueprint expansion** — the chapter's proof sketch is likely under-specified; the planner should expand it (via the appropriate blueprint-writing subagent in their catalog) before more prover work.
215	- **Mathlib analogy consult** — the project may be using a parallel API or wrong predicate; the planner should consult Mathlib-idiom analysis on the route's load-bearing definitions.
216	- **Refactor** — the definition or file structure may be wrong; the planner should dispatch a structural subagent to restructure before more prover work.
217	- **Route pivot** — the strategic route may be wrong entirely; the planner should revise STRATEGY.md and pick a different route, then re-run any strategy critic in the catalog mid-iter to validate the pivot.
218	- **User escalation** — none of the above will work; the planner should pause and request user input. Use sparingly — only when no automated corrective will resolve the stall.
219	
220	Pick ONE primary corrective per CHURNING/STUCK route. Multiple are allowed when truly necessary, listed in priority order.
221	
222	## Report format
223	
224	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/progress-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
225	
226	**Omit-empty rule.** Only `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict` are required. Omit any section whose right answer is "nothing to report" — do NOT write "none", "N/A", or "no findings" as filler. The absence of a section IS the signal. Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is CONVERGING with no recurring blockers and no secondary correctives, the block may be the trajectory + status pattern + verdict line only; drop the empty-list fields. Same for dispatch sanity: if dispatch is OK, render `## PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity` as a single line (`Verdict: OK — file count <N> within cap <C>; no growth-while-churning`) rather than a multi-line block with empty sub-fields.
227	
228	```markdown
229	# Progress Critic Report
230	
231	## Slug
232	<slug>
233	
234	## Iteration
235	<NNN>
236	
237	## Routes audited
238	
239	For each route in the directive, one block:
240	
241	### Route: <name>
242	
243	- **Sorry trajectory**: <description, e.g. "5 → 5 → 4 → 4 → 4 across iter-100 to 104">
244	- **Helper accumulation**: <description, e.g. "13 helpers added across last 4 iters; 1 sorry closed">
245	- **Recurring blockers**: <list or "none">
246	- **Prover status pattern**: <e.g. "PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL">
247	- **Throughput**: ON_SCHEDULE | SLIPPING | OVER_BUDGET | ESTIMATE_FREE — <"estimated K iters, elapsed K'; estimate honest | dishonest | absent">
248	- **Verdict**: CONVERGING | CHURNING | STUCK | UNCLEAR
249	- **Primary corrective** (if CHURNING/STUCK): <one of the actions above, with one paragraph of why>
250	- **Secondary correctives** (if applicable): <list>
251	
252	## PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity
253	
254	Independent of any route verdict — operates on the planner's current `## Current Objectives` proposal as a whole.
255	
256	- **File count**: <N> (cap: <C>)
257	- **Over the cap**: yes | no — <if yes: list the files beyond the cap that the planner must defer>
258	- **Iter-over-iter trend**: <e.g. "4 → 7 → 12; growing while route signals say CHURNING">
259	- **Verdict**: OK | OVER_CAP | BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS
260	  - OK: dispatch list is within cap and not growing while routes churn.
261	  - OVER_CAP: planner listed more files than the cap allows. The deterministic guard will truncate, but the planner must self-correct — picking 27 files to dispatch is a planning failure, not a tooling failure. Land this in must-fix-this-iter.
262	  - BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS: file count growing iter over iter while route signals say CHURNING/STUCK. Strong "throwing provers at the wall" signature. Land this in must-fix-this-iter.
263	
264	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if no CHURNING/STUCK verdicts AND no OVER_BUDGET/OVER_CAP/BLOAT findings -->
265	
266	Every CHURNING and every STUCK verdict lands here automatically, every OVER_BUDGET throughput finding (with `Iters left > 0`), and every OVER_CAP / BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS dispatch verdict. Do not under-classify.
267	
268	- Route <name>: <verdict> — primary corrective: <action>. Why: <one line>.
269	- Route <name>: OVER_BUDGET throughput — STRATEGY.md estimates <K> iters, elapsed <K'>. Revise the estimate or escalate (pivot / narrow scope).
270	- Dispatch: OVER_CAP — planner listed <N> files (cap <C>). Re-prioritize this iter; defer <N-C> files.
271	- Dispatch: BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS — file count <a> → <b> → <c> while routes <X>, <Y> remain CHURNING. Stop adding more files; address the churn first.
272	- ...
273	
274	## Informational <!-- omit if every route is CONVERGING with no commentary worth surfacing; the per-route block above already carries the verdict -->
275	
276	CONVERGING and UNCLEAR verdicts that warrant a comment. The planner reads these but they don't gate the iter. If every route is cleanly CONVERGING with the verdict line above being sufficient, omit this section.
277	
278	## Overall verdict
279	
280	One paragraph: how many routes are healthy, how many are stuck, what the planner's iter should look like to address the stuck ones.
281	```
282	
283	## Return value
284	
285	Your final assistant message:
286	
287	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHURNING/STUCK verdicts, dispatch=<OK|OVER_CAP|BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS>`
288	- The path to your full report.
289	
290	## Reminders
291	
292	- **You don't read strategy or blueprint.** Convergence is the question; soundness is for other subagents.
293	- **No bias toward CONVERGING.** The planner wants the route to be CONVERGING; you do not. Apply the verdict rules verbatim.
294	- **One primary corrective per route.** Don't list five and let the planner pick.
295	- **Recurring blockers are signal, not noise.** When the same blocker phrase appears across 3+ iters, the route is stuck regardless of helper counts.
296

57	
58	# Strategy Critic
59	
60	You are the fresh-context strategy critic. You read the project's `STRATEGY.md` with **no exposure to its iter-by-iter history** and challenge the strategic choices as if a mathematician encountering this project for the first time would.
61	
62	Your job is to be the project's adversarial reader. The plan agent has been in the project's context for many iterations and is naturally invested in the existing routes. You are the corrective.
63	
64	## Your context discipline
65	
66	Your directive will name ONLY:
67	
68	- The current `STRATEGY.md` (the primary subject of your review).
69	- The project's stated final goal (one paragraph naming the theorem(s) to formalize).
70	- A short reference index (`references/summary.md` content).
71	- A blueprint summary (chapter titles + one-line topic per chapter).
72	
73	Your directive will NOT include `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, prover task results, review summaries, or recent narrative. **If the directive accidentally includes any of these, ignore them.** Your value depends entirely on the lack of pollution. Reading the iter-by-iter history would make you the planner; the planner already exists. Stay fresh.
74	
75	You may use:
76	
77	- The references named in the directive (read them if needed for the math).
78	- The blueprint chapter summaries (the prose summary in the directive, NOT the full chapter text — full-chapter audit is the territory of the blueprint-review subagent in the catalog).
79	- `archon-lean-lsp` if you need to spot-check a Mathlib name's existence.
80	
81	You may NOT use:
82	
83	- Iter sidecars, even if you find their paths.
84	- Any state file other than what the directive named.
85	
86	## What you check
87	
88	For each strategic route in `STRATEGY.md`:
89	
90	1. **Goal-alignment.** Does this route, if executed, actually produce the project's stated final goal? Or is there a subtle gap where the route's end-state isn't quite what the goal demands?
91	
92	2. **Mathematical soundness.** Is the route's mathematical argument plausible? Are there steps that look like they assume something the route's prerequisites don't give you? A fresh reader is good at spotting "wait, this step needs X but X isn't established."
93	
94	3. **Alternative routes.** Are there other approaches the strategy doesn't mention? For each, briefly note: what it would look like, why it might be cheaper or sounder, and why the strategy may have rejected it (if you can tell from prose).
95	
96	4. **Sunk-cost reasoning.** Does the strategy justify a route in terms of "we've already done X" rather than "X is the right way"? That's a sunk-cost smell. Call it out by name.
97	
98	5. **Prerequisite assumptions.** Does the strategy assume Mathlib infrastructure that may not exist? Verify the named lemmas / type classes / structures actually exist (use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for spot-checks). Strategy that depends on phantom Mathlib infra is invalid.
99	
100	6. **Effort estimates.** If the strategy carries per-route LOC or iteration estimates, do they look honest given the scope of the route? Estimates that are wildly under-counted (e.g. "200 LOC for representability of Pic") indicate either underestimated effort or a misplanned route. The LOC cell carries two figures (`remaining · realized/it`); flag rows where they are internally inconsistent with `Iters left` — e.g. `≈250 · ~30/it` alongside `Iters left: 2` is arithmetically impossible (250 ÷ 30 ≈ 8), a dishonest-estimate signal — and rows reading `~0/it` that are still claimed as actively progressing.
101	
102	7. **Format compliance.** `STRATEGY.md` must follow the canonical skeleton documented in the plan prompt. Violations to flag:
103	
104	   - **Size**: the file exceeds ~250 lines or ~12 KB.
105	   - **Headings**: the section list isn't exactly `## Goal`, `## Phases & estimations`, `## Routes`, `## Open strategic questions`, `## Mathlib gaps & new material` (in that order). Renamed or extra top-level sections (`## Project goal`, `## End-state`, `## Decomposition`, `## Roadmap`, `## Soundness rules`, etc.) are violations.
106	   - **Per-iter narrative**: references to specific iterations ("iter-NNN", "this iter we tried X", "last iter", "the iter-XYZ pivot"). Per-iter history belongs in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, never in STRATEGY.md.
107	   - **No accumulation**: completed phases or excised routes still occupy space. The file must shrink toward "complete", not grow.
108	   - **Table discipline**: `## Phases & estimations` must be a Markdown table with columns Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (remaining · realized/it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks, one short line per cell. The LOC cell must carry both the remaining-LOC estimate and the realized per-iter velocity (e.g. `≈250 · ~30/it`); a LOC cell with only one figure is a (minor) discipline gap. Long prose in cells, or replacing the table with prose subsections, is a violation.
109	   - **Appendix sections**: "Historical decisions", "Considered alternatives", "Past iterations summary", "Lessons learned", or any other history-tracking section. Iter sidecars are where rejected alternatives live.
110	
111	   Format violations are reported under a synthetic "format" route — see the Report Format section below. **Format is not cosmetic.** A STRATEGY.md that drifts from the canonical skeleton bleeds into the plan agent's context every iter and makes the strategy itself harder to reason about. Treat material format violations as a CHALLENGE that must be resolved this iter via an in-place restructure (using iter sidecars to hold any historical detail that currently lives inline).
112	
113	## Directive Format
114	
115	```markdown
116	# Strategy Critic Directive
117	
118	## Slug
119	<slug>
120	
121	## Project goal
122	<one paragraph: the final theorem(s) or claim(s) the project is trying to formalize. Include the protected declarations if any.>
123	
124	## Strategy under review
125	
126	<paste the entire STRATEGY.md verbatim.>
127	
128	## References index
129	
130	<paste references/summary.md verbatim.>
131	
132	## Blueprint summary
133	
134	<for each blueprint chapter, one line: filename + topic. NOT the chapter content — just the index.>
135	
136	## Prior critique status
137	
138	<one line per still-live prior-iter challenge. Format STRICTLY:
139	
140	  - <prior-iter-NNN>: <short challenge phrase> — live | addressed
141	
142	NO paraphrased planner responses, NO multi-paragraph re-litigation,
143	NO "the iter-XXX plan-agent considered pulling this to iter-YYY but
144	deferred to iter-ZZZ" — those are iter-by-iter narrative and would
145	contaminate the fresh-context audit. The point of "Prior critique
146	status" is solely to tell you which challenges to re-check, NOT how
147	the planner reasoned about them. If a prior critique is fully resolved
148	in the current STRATEGY.md, mark it "addressed"; if you disagree
149	after auditing, your report will flip it back to live.
150	
151	If first-iter (no prior critique), write "no prior critique".>
152	```
153	
154	**If you find this section contains planner-side reasoning, iter
155	narrative, or paraphrased plan-agent responses beyond the one-line
156	"live | addressed" tags, ignore that extra material.** Your fresh
157	context is your value; do not let leaked planner state colonize
158	your audit.
159	
160	## Report format
161	
162	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/strategy-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
163	
164	**Omit-empty rule.** Every section below is optional except `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict`. If a section's right answer is "nothing to report", **OMIT the section entirely** — do NOT write "none", "N/A", "no findings detected", or "(omit if empty)" as filler content. The absence of a section IS the signal that nothing was found there; a `## Sunk-cost flags` heading with "(none detected)" underneath is bloat, not signal. Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is SOUND with no flagged items, you may render the block as just the verdict line (e.g. `### Route: <name>\n- **Verdict**: SOUND — strategy is internally consistent and matches the project's goal`) and omit the bullet checklist above it. Filling templates with hollow content is exactly the failure mode this rule exists to avoid.
165	
166	```markdown
167	# Strategy Critic Report
168	
169	## Slug
170	<slug>
171	
172	## Iteration
173	<NNN>
174	
175	## Routes audited
176	
177	For each strategic route in STRATEGY.md, one block:
178	
179	### Route: <name>
180	
181	- **Goal-alignment**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
182	- **Mathematical soundness**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
183	- **Sunk-cost reasoning detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name the sunk-cost claim verbatim>
184	- **Phantom prerequisites**: <list any Mathlib infra the strategy assumes exists that you couldn't verify>
185	- **Effort honesty**: <reasonable | under-counted | over-counted> — <one line>
186	- **Verdict**: SOUND | CHALLENGE | REJECT
187	  - SOUND: the route makes sense and the planner should proceed.
188	  - CHALLENGE: the route has issues the planner must address (in STRATEGY.md or via an explicit rebuttal in plan.md) before this iter ends.
189	  - REJECT: the route is fundamentally broken (goal-misaligned, mathematically unsound, or built on phantom prerequisites). Do not proceed on this route until the strategy is rewritten.
190	
191	## Format compliance
192	
193	A separate block from "Routes audited" — this audits the *document* against the canonical skeleton, regardless of whether the strategic content is sound. Format and content are orthogonal: a strategy can be sound but poorly formatted, or well-formatted but unsound.
194	
195	- **Size**: <line count> / <bytes> — within budget | over budget (~250 lines / ~12 KB).
196	- **Headings**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, list the headings that violate the canonical set, e.g. "extra `## Roadmap`, missing `## Phases & estimations`, renamed `## Goal` → `## Project goal`">.
197	- **Per-iter narrative detected**: yes | no — <if yes, quote one or two representative phrases verbatim>.
198	- **Accumulation detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name the completed phases / excised routes still present>.
199	- **Table discipline**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, describe how `## Phases & estimations` deviates from the table-with-named-columns shape>.
200	- **Appendix sections**: <list any "Historical", "Considered alternatives", "Past summary", etc. sections detected; omit if none>.
201	- **Format verdict**: COMPLIANT | DRIFTED | NON-COMPLIANT
202	  - COMPLIANT: minor or no deviations.
203	  - DRIFTED: multiple deviations but core skeleton intact; planner should clean up this iter without a full restructure.
204	  - NON-COMPLIANT: the document doesn't follow the skeleton. The planner MUST restructure STRATEGY.md in-place this iter, moving any per-iter narrative or appendix content to iter sidecars. This is a CHALLENGE-level finding; do not under-classify.
205	
206	## Alternative routes (suggested) <!-- omit entire section if no fresh alternatives -->
207	
208	For each suggested alternative the strategy doesn't mention, one block:
209	
210	### Alternative: <name>
211	
212	- **What it looks like**: <one paragraph>
213	- **Why it might be cheaper or sounder**: <one paragraph>
214	- **What the current strategy may have rejected**: <if guessable from prose; otherwise "unclear, planner should clarify">
215	- **Severity of the omission**: critical | major | minor
216	
217	## Sunk-cost flags <!-- omit entire section if no sunk-cost reasoning detected -->
218	
219	For every instance of "we have already X, so we should continue with Y" reasoning in the strategy:
220	
221	- `<verbatim quote>` — Why this is sunk-cost: <one sentence>. Recommendation: <reframe the decision on its merits, not its history>.
222	
223	## Prerequisite verification <!-- omit entire section if strategy named no specific Mathlib infrastructure to verify -->
224	
225	Each Mathlib infrastructure piece the strategy named:
226	
227	- `<Mathlib name>`: VERIFIED (exists) | MISSING (couldn't locate) | RENAMED (exists under different name X)
228	
229	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if zero CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts AND format is COMPLIANT -->
230	
231	Every CHALLENGE and every REJECT verdict lands here, and every NON-COMPLIANT format verdict lands here. Apply verbatim, no under-classification:
232	
233	- Route <name>: CHALLENGE — <what the planner must address>.
234	- Alternative <name>: critical — strategy ignored a cheaper / sounder route. Planner must address.
235	- Phantom prerequisite <name>: strategy depends on a Mathlib piece I couldn't verify.
236	- Format: NON-COMPLIANT — STRATEGY.md must be restructured in-place this iter. <list the two or three most impactful deviations>. Move per-iter narrative and appendix content to `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
237	
238	## Overall verdict
239	
240	One paragraph: would a fresh mathematician approve this strategy as-is, or are there material concerns?
241	```
242	
243	## Return value
244	
245	Your final assistant message:
246	
247	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts, format=<COMPLIANT|DRIFTED|NON-COMPLIANT>`
248	- The path to your full report.
249	
250	## Reminders
251	
252	- **You are the project's adversarial reader.** Don't be polite. If a route is sunk-cost reasoning dressed up as strategy, say so.
253	- **Don't request more context.** Iter history is what you're meant to be free of.
254	- **Cite Mathlib precisely.** Use the LSP tools to verify before claiming a prerequisite exists or is missing.
255	- **Strict severity.** CHALLENGE and REJECT are must-fix; do not under-classify to keep momentum.
256	- **Be specific about alternatives.** "Maybe consider another approach" is useless. Name it.
257

# Strategy Critic Directive

## Slug
overbudget-recheck

## Project goal

Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`): nine protected
declarations, headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and `Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` —
the existence of an Albanese / Jacobian object uniform over the $k$-rational pointing of a smooth
proper geometrically irreducible curve $C/k$, with **no** $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ hypothesis on the
protected signature. The witness OBJECT $J$ is always real; only `isAlbaneseFor` is universally
quantified over $P : \mathbf 1 \to C$. `genus C := \dim_k H^1(C, O_C)` (arithmetic genus, protected).
End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms.

## Strategy under review

# Strategy

## Goal

Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
nine protected declarations, headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — the existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
object uniform over the $k$-rational pointing of a smooth proper geometrically
irreducible curve `C/k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis on the protected
signature. End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms.

The protected signature quantifies over arbitrary `C` with no $C(k) \neq \emptyset$
assumption. The witness OBJECT `J` is always real (constructed unconditionally);
only `isAlbaneseFor` is universally quantified over `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`. The spine is
**pointed vs. unpointed**, not genus-0 vs. positive. `genus C := dim_k H^1(C,O_C)`
(arithmetic genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).

## Phases & estimations

| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (remaining · realized/it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Route A — Picard/Quot/Hilbert FGA engine** (witness OBJECT for positive genus; CRITICAL PATH) | committed, blueprint sketch-level | many (~40–70) | ~5100+ · 0/it | Hilbert/Quot representability (Nitsure §4–5); FGA `Pic_{C/k}` repr; identity component `Pic⁰`; Albanese universal property | A.2 representability is the riskiest, least-Mathlib-supported piece; smallest entry `RelativeSpec` ~700–1100 LOC. Project-fatal if it stalls — but mandatory regardless (the positive-genus object is `Pic⁰`) |
| **genus-0 rigidity** (committed route (c); object `J=Spec k` already trivial) | Rigidity-Lemma chain CLOSING (iter-162: lone deep Step-1 residual, prover recipe in hand); cube + RR + ℙ¹→A + headline all UNSTARTED ahead. NEW home: own upstream file/chapter `AbelianVarietyRigidity` (breaks the import cycle) | **re-est ~18–32 (was ~10–18; OVER_BUDGET confirmed iter-162)** — cube ~8–15 + genus-0⟹ℙ¹ RR ~5–10, both zero-Mathlib, dominate; chain ≈12 elapsed | ~3500–6500 · chain ~40/it, cube+RR 0/it | (i) **Rigidity Lemma** (Mumford Form I §4 — CUBE-FREE, CLOSING iter-162); (ii) **theorem of the cube** (Mumford §6/§10 — REQUIRED; seesaw + flat/proper cohomology base-change + semicontinuity + line bundles on products); (iii) ℙ¹→A const (Milne Prop 3.10, induction cube-free via Cor 1.5; base case via cube); (iv) genus-0+k̄-pt ⟹ ≅ℙ¹ (Hartshorne IV.1.3.5, Riemann–Roch — **no Mathlib RR**) | cube DOMINATES — comparable to a chunk of representability; SHARED with Route A's Albanese UP (Milne §III.6), so NOT throwaway. The RR genus-0⟹ℙ¹ bridge is a SECOND major unstarted sub-build. Re-est licensed by >30% change (cube+RR at 0/it). rigidity_lemma closes early but is necessary-not-sufficient. Decisive reason for (c): char-freeness (see Routes) |
| `genusZeroWitness` body + terminal cluster on `Spec k` + **`k̄→k` descent** | gated | 3–5 | 350–850 · gated | terminal-object cluster on `Spec k`; **faithfully-flat descent of morphism equality** along `Spec k̄ → Spec k` (route-dependent: a `Pic⁰`-over-`k` argument may avoid it; a `C_k̄≅ℙ¹` argument incurs it) | descent right-cancellation direction unconfirmed; skeleton already built (6/7 fields closed, only the `key` rigidity equation open) |
| **char-`p` genus-0 rigidity** (protected goal over arbitrary `[Field k]`, no `CharZero`) | gated; may dissolve under route (c) | ? | ? · 0 | none if the rigidity lemma is char-free (it is); a Frobenius-descent plan only if the differential fallback (a) is used | the differential fallback carries `[CharZero k]`; the rigidity-lemma route (c) is char-free, likely removing this gap entirely — confirm when route (c) is settled |
| `nonempty_jacobianWitness` genus-stratified body | gated | 1 | <50 · gated | `by_cases h : genus C = 0` | trivial once both arms close |

## Routes

The witness OBJECT for positive genus is built by **Route A (Picard scheme via
FGA)** — mandatory and essentially unavoidable. The genus-0 arm is a separate,
lower-risk rigidity statement (object trivial); its proof route is under
determination, with two fallbacks held live.

### Route A (COMMITTED) — Picard scheme / Albanese via FGA

Hilbert/Quot representability + identity-component `Pic⁰` + Albanese universal
property. Decomposition A.1–A.4 in `Jacobian.tex` § "Route A". Literature in-tree:
`references/kleiman-picard.pdf` (§4 existence, §5 `Pic⁰`), `references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.pdf`
(Quot/Hilbert engine).

**Positive-genus arm:** `J := Pic⁰_{C/k}`, a dim-`g` abelian variety; the Albanese
UP gives `isAlbaneseFor`. This is the project's critical path: the object must be a
real dim-`g` abelian variety even when `C(k)=∅`, so the FGA engine is required
unconditionally.

### Genus-0 rigidity — the keystone `rigidity_over_kbar`, decoupled

The genus-0 witness OBJECT is the trivial `J = Spec k` (skeleton already 6/7
closed); it needs **no** Picard scheme. What remains is a single rigidity
STATEMENT: over `k̄`, a genus-0 curve is `≅ ℙ¹`, and any pointed `f : C → A` killing
`P` is constant. Three candidate proof routes, ranked:

- **(c) [COMMITTED] targeted char-free AV-rigidity lemma.** "Every morphism from a
  genus-0 curve (`≅ ℙ¹` over `k̄`) to an abelian variety is constant." **Minimal chain**
  (sources now in tree — Mumford + Hartshorne + Milne): (i) **Rigidity Lemma** (Mumford
  *Abelian Varieties*, Form I, §4 p.43; = Milne Thm 1.1) — `V` complete, `V×W→Z` trivial
  on `V×{w0}` ⟹ factors through `W`; this is **CUBE-FREE** (a properness/closedness
  argument, NOT the theorem of the cube). (ii) `ℙ¹ → A` constant (Milne Prop 3.10's
  packaged corollary; Mumford leaves it as a consequence of the rigidity lemma). (iii)
  genus-0 + `k̄`-point ⟹ `≅ ℙ¹` (Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 / Ex IV.1.3, Riemann–Roch-flavoured).
  Uses NEITHER Serre duality NOR Picard representability. **Decisive reason (c) beats the
  cheap-ℙ¹ `df=0` route:** over `k̄`, `H^0(ℙ¹,Ω)=H^0(ℙ¹,O(−2))=0` is *elementary* (the
  iter-155 chart-by-chart refutation does NOT touch the concrete `ℙ¹`), so `df=0` is reachable
  — but `df=0 ⟹ constant` is **FALSE in char `p`** without Frobenius descent. The protected
  goal is arbitrary `[Field k]`, so char-freeness is decisive. **Cube question RESOLVED
  iter-157** (NEGATIVE): the split is opposite the iter-156 hope — the multi-factor induction
  (Cor 1.5) is cube-free, but the *base case* "`ℙ¹→A` constant" (an AV has no rational curves)
  rests irreducibly on the cube. So `rigidity_lemma` is cube-free + prover-ready but does NOT
  alone close the arm; the cube is the heavy keystone, SHARED with Route A's Albanese UP (not
  throwaway). (c) stays cheaper than (b) (= cube AND representability). Decoupled from FGA.
- **(b) byproduct of the engine.** Once Route A exists, `Alb(genus-0)=Pic⁰(ℙ¹)=0`,
  so `f` factors through `Spec k̄`. Free, but couples genus-0 to A.2 representability
  — only attractive if (c) proves infeasible.
- **(a) fallback — differential route via Serre duality.** `df=0` via {`Ω_A`
  cotangent-triviality (~800–1500) + Serre duality `H^0(C,Ω_C)=0` (~3000–8000)},
  REUSING the closed, axiom-clean chart-algebra envelope (KDM,
  `constants_integral_over_base_field`, `df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart`)
  which supplies the converse `df=0 ⟹ constant`. **Kept in tree, off path** — not
  deleted. Carries a char-`p` gap. Reversal signal: if (c) is infeasible AND Route
  A's A.2 stalls, this decouples genus-0 from FGA risk.

# Blueprint Writer Directive

## Chapter
blueprint/src/chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex

## Slug
avr-helpers

## Strategy context (the slice that matters)

The committed genus-0 route (route (c)) lives in this chapter. The Rigidity-Lemma chain
(`thm:rigidity_lemma → lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open → lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine →
{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints (proven), lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine
(residual)}`) is one residual `sorry` from being fully proven. This iter the prover closes the lone
Step-1 residual `lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine`. Two Lean declarations need
blueprint nodes so the dependency graph reflects the on-disk Lean truthfully.

Your job is TWO additive edits to this chapter. Do NOT rewrite existing blocks, do NOT touch the
already-proven chain lemmas' statements, do NOT touch the "theorem of the cube" section (a separate
future task), do NOT touch any protected signature, and do NOT add or remove any `\leanok` /
`\mathlibok` marker (those are managed by the deterministic sync / review agent — leave all marker
concerns out entirely).

## Edit 1 — add a node for the PROVEN algebraic-core helper `eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral`

This resolves the live iter-161 lean-vs-blueprint-checker MAJOR: the reusable, axiom-clean,
substantive Lean helper `AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral` has NO `\lean{}`
node and no `\uses` edge anywhere in the chapter (documentary gap; the chapter even carries a
`% NOTE (iter-161 review)` TODO at the proof of `lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine`
asking for exactly this). Add a short `\begin{lemma}...\end{lemma}` node, placed immediately BEFORE
`lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine` (so the `\uses` runs forward), and then wire
that lemma's proof `\uses{}` to the new label.

**The new lemma's mathematical content** (this is a project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas — it
realizes, cohomology-free, the classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over
an algebraically closed field is constant", which is the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity
Lemma proof already quoted in this chapter; NO new external source quote is needed — present it as a
project-bespoke lemma, citing in prose that it realizes that classical step):

- **Label:** `lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral`.
- **`\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}`**.
- **Statement.** Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $W$ an integral scheme equipped with a
  structure morphism $w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k$ that is universally closed (proper) and locally of
  finite type. Let $g \colon W \to V$ be a morphism into an affine scheme $V$. Then any two
  $\bar k$-points of $W$ — i.e. any two sections $a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W$ of $w$ — satisfy
  $a \mathbin{;} g = b \mathbin{;} g$ (`a ≫ g = b ≫ g`).
- **Proof prose.** Because $W$ is integral and $w$ is universally closed, the global sections
  $\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)$ form a field (`isField_of_universallyClosed`), and the structure ring map
  $w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)$ (i.e. `wk.appTop` after the canonical $\Gamma(\Spec \bar k)
  \cong \bar k$ identification) is integral / module-finite (`finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`).
  Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $\Gamma(W)$ is a domain finite over it, that map is
  bijective (`IsAlgClosed.ringHom_bijective_of_isIntegral`), so $\Gamma(W) = \bar k$ and $w^\sharp$ is
  an isomorphism — in particular an epimorphism on $\Gamma$. Both sections $a, b$ left-invert
  $w^\sharp$ on global sections, so they induce the same ring map $a^\sharp = b^\sharp$ on
  $\Gamma(W)$ (cancel the epi $w^\sharp$). A morphism into the AFFINE $V$ is pinned by its global-
  sections ring map (`ext_of_isAffine`), so $a \mathbin{;} g = b \mathbin{;} g$.
- **Every hypothesis is load-bearing** (the iter-161 audit confirmed): `IsAlgClosed` collapses the
  finite extension; `IsIntegral`/integral $W$ makes $\Gamma(W)$ a field (else a two-point
  counterexample); `UniversallyClosed` is what makes $\Gamma$ a field (else $\mathbb A^1$ is a
  counterexample); `LocallyOfFiniteType` gives finiteness of $\Gamma$ over $\bar k$; `IsAffine V` is
  what lets `ext_of_isAffine` pin the map. State these in one sentence.

Then add `lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral` to the `\uses{}` of the PROOF block of
`lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine` (it currently has none on its proof). Replace
the existing `% NOTE (iter-161 review) ... PLAN/BLUEPRINT-WRITER TODO: add a ...node` comment block
(at the Step-1 proof) with a one-line note that the node is now added, OR simply delete that TODO
comment since it is resolved.

## Edit 2 — document the retract-integrality fact `IsIntegral X.left` (the lone remaining Step-1 sub-build)

The prover's iter-161 report identifies the single remaining geometric blocker inside
`lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine`: realising the slice $X_y \cong X$ as proper
INTEGRAL requires `IsIntegral X.left`, which is NOT automatic and must be derived from $X$ being a
retract of the integral product $X \times Y$. The prover will build this as a named top-level helper
this iter. Give it a short blueprint node (a `\begin{lemma}...\end{lemma}`, project-bespoke, NO
external source) so the dependency graph covers it, placed near / before
`lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine`:

- **Label:** `lem:isIntegral_of_retract_of_integral` (or a clearer name of your choosing; record it).
- Leave the `\lean{...}` cross-ref to the name the prover will use IF you can predict it; otherwise
  state the lemma WITHOUT a `\lean{}` block (a prose lemma the prover will name), and note in a
  `% NOTE:` that the `\lean{}` cross-ref should be filled once the prover lands the helper. Do not
  fabricate a `\lean{}` name.
- **Mathematical content.** Let $X \times Y$ be integral (reduced + irreducible — here it is
  geometrically irreducible and reduced). Suppose $X$ is a retract of $X \times Y$: there is a section
  $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ with $s \mathbin{;} p_1 = \mathbf 1_X$ ($p_1$ the first projection).
  Then $X$ is integral. Proof: $X$ is reduced because $p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X \to (p_1)_*
  \mathcal O_{X \times Y}$ (equivalently the section's split injection on rings) embeds $\mathcal O_X$
  into the reduced $\mathcal O_{X \times Y}$ (`isReduced_of_injective` on the split-injective ring
  map); $X$ is irreducible because it is the continuous image of the irreducible $X \times Y$ under
  the surjection $p_1$ (a surjection because it has the section $s$; continuous image of an
  irreducible space is irreducible). Reduced + irreducible = integral.
- This is elementary (no external citation); present as a project-bespoke lemma feeding the Step-1
  geometric assembly. Add it to the Step-1 proof's `\uses{}` alongside Edit 1's label.

## Out of scope (do NOT touch)

- The "theorem of the cube" section (§ deferred deep input) — a separate, larger future blueprint
  task; leave it exactly as is.
- The already-proven chain-lemma statement blocks (`thm:rigidity_lemma`, `lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`,
  `lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine`, `lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints`) — do not
  restate or re-sign them.
- All `\leanok` / `\mathlibok` markers — do not add or remove any.
- Protected signatures (none of these chain lemmas is protected, but do not alter signatures).

## Citation discipline reminder

Both new lemmas are project-bespoke (assemblies of existing Mathlib lemmas / elementary topology +
algebra), so they carry NO `% SOURCE:` / `% SOURCE QUOTE:` blocks — they stand on their proof prose
alone, exactly like the existing `lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints` block. Do not fabricate a
citation. You MAY note in prose that Edit 1's lemma realizes the "proper connected variety into an
affine is a single point" step already quoted verbatim from Mumford elsewhere in the chapter.

Reuses the **already-built, axiom-clean** chart-algebra envelope + GrpObj template.
50	- **Why it might be cheaper or sounder**: it avoids the theorem of the cube and its entire drag (seesaw, flat/proper cohomology base-change, semicontinuity, line bundles on products) — the strategy's own DOMINANT, "comparable-to-representability" cost. The only genus-0-specific new build is relative-Frobenius factorization for smooth curves, a far more contained piece than the cube apparatus, plus the `Ω_A` globalization that is already templated. The strategy demoted the differential route (a) citing "`df=0 ⟹ const` is FALSE in char `p`" — but it simultaneously names the patch ("Frobenius descent") and **never costs it**, so the dismissal is unquantified.
51	- **What the current strategy may have rejected**: the strategy treats the cube as "irreducible" (RESOLVED iter-157, NEGATIVE) and froze it ("No budget cut"). But "irreducible" is contingent on *refusing the differential approach*, not a theorem: the `df=0+Frobenius` path is a genuine cube-free route to the same base case. The strategy's "irreducible" claim conflates "no cube-free *non-differential* argument" with "no cube-free argument."
52	- **Severity of the omission**: major — it directly attacks the ~8–15-iter cost the OVER_BUDGET re-estimate is built on.

This computes `Alb=0` from the *genus* directly and **never needs the `C_k̄≅ℙ¹` RR bridge** (the ~5–10-iter genus-0-specific cost), nor a separate cube-wiring, nor the rigidity-lemma chain.
57	- **Why it might be cheaper or sounder**: Route A is mandatory regardless. If the cube is genuinely shared with Route A's Albanese UP (the strategy's own claim), then route (b) costs essentially ZERO genus-0-specific iters beyond "a 0-dim'l connected group scheme over a field is trivial." Paying 18–32 iters to AVOID coupling to a phase that is being built anyway is a steep insurance premium.
58	- **What the current strategy may have rejected**: coupling to A.2 representability risk. But note the decoupling logic is **partially self-defeating**: route (c) *also* needs the cube, so if Route A stalls *at the cube*, (c) stalls too. (c)'s insurance only covers the *post-cube A.2-specific* failure mode — a narrower risk than advertised, for a large premium.
59	- **Severity of the omission**: major — the budget doubling is exactly the event that should re-open (b)-vs-(c), and the strategy declares "No budget cut" instead of re-deciding on merits.
60
